sections.heading: Why idioms about time?
sections.body: Time is one of the most common conceptual domains in any language. English has an unusually rich set of idioms for time — many come from sailing, agriculture, and the rhythms of pre-industrial life.

sections.heading: The most useful ones
sections.body: 'In the nick of time' — just before it's too late. 'Once in a blue moon' — very rarely (blue moons are rare astronomical events). 'Against the clock' — rushing because of a deadline. 'Around the clock' — continuously, 24 hours. 'In no time (at all)' — very quickly.

examples.english: We caught the last train in the nick of time.
examples.translation: justo a tiempo — por muy poco

examples.english: She calls her parents once in a blue moon.
The team was working against the clock.
The hospital is staffed around the clock.
Don't worry, I'll finish it in no time.

exercises.instruction: Replace the underlined words with a time idiom.
items: She arrived just before the meeting started.
They finished the report very quickly.

quiz.question: 'Once in a blue moon' means:
quiz.explanation: A blue moon is a rare astronomical event — the idiom means something happens very infrequently.
